نبھانا.e: Ultimately from Sanskrit निर्वाहयति (nirvāhayati). Compare Punjabi ਨਿਭਾਉਣਾ (nibhāuṇā) / نِبھاؤݨا (nibhā'oṇā), Sindhi نِڀائِڻُ (nibhāiṇu) / निभाइणु, Gujarati નિભાવવું (nibhāvvũ), Marathi निभावणे (nibhāvṇe).

نتیجتا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نتیجتا (natījatan), from Arabic نَتِيجَةً (natījatan), adverbial accusative of نَتِيجَة (natīja).

نتیجہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نتیجه (natīja), from Arabic نَتِيجَة (natīja). Compare Sindhi نتيجو (natījo).

نجار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَجَّار (najjār), borrowed from Arabic نَجّار (najjār).

senses.g: the noble; the excellent, etc.; nobles; heroes
any plant without a stalk or trunk, such as grass

نجم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نجم (najm), from Arabic نَجْم (najm).

نجوم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نجوم (nujūm), from Arabic نُجُوم (nujūm).

senses.g: of noble birth, noble, praiseworthy, honourable
a class of Indian soldiers (who are distinguished by a peculiar dress), irregular troops
to extract by squeezing, squeeze out
to extract, exact, wrest (something of value, usually money)

نچوڑنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀦𑀺𑀘𑁆𑀘𑁄𑀟𑁂𑀤𑀺 (*niccoḍedi), from Sanskrit *निश्चोटयति (*niścoṭayati), from निस्- (nis-, “out”) + चुट् (cuṭ, “to cut off”).

نحوست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نحوست (nuhūsat), from Arabic نُحُوسَة (nuḥūsa).

نخچیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَخْچِیر (naxčīr, “hunt, hunting; game, quarry; prey”). Cognate with Parthian 𐭍𐭇𐭔𐭉𐭓 (naxčīr, “game, quarry, chase”), Sogdian ܢܓܫܝܪ (naxšīr, “wild beast; wild goat”), Wakhi نخچیر (naxčīr, “fox”), Yidgha نخشیر (naxšīr, “goat”), Yaghnobi нахшир (naxšir), Sanskrit नश्चीरपति (naścīrapati), and Gandhari 𐨣𐨐𐨿𐨕𐨁𐨌𐨪 (naxcīra).

نخچیر زن.e: From نخچیر (naxcīr, “prey; game, quarry”) + زن (zan, “slay, kill; strike”), literally "game-slayer".

نخچیر کشی.e: From نخچیر (naxcīr, “prey; game, quarry”) + کشی (kušī, “slaying, killing”).

نخچیر گاہ.e: From نخچیر (naxcīr, “prey; game, quarry”) + گاہ (gāh, “place, center”), literally “place of prey”.

senses.g: one who sacrifices themselves in the name of love; one who is a victim of love and passion

نخچیر محبت.e: From نخچیر (naxcīr, “prey; quarry”) + محبت (muhabbat, “love, affection”), literally "prey of passion" or "victim of love".

نخست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نُخُسْت (nuxust). Compare روزِ نُخُسْت (roz-i nuxust) and Hindi रोज़-ए-नुख़ुस्त (roz-e-nuxust).

senses.g: oasis (spring of fresh water, surrounded by a fertile region of vegetation, in a desert)

نڈر.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi نڐر (nḍr) (c. 1609), from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀡𑀺𑀟𑁆𑀟𑀭 (*ṇiḍḍara), from Sanskrit निर्दर (nirdara, “pitiless, *fearless”). Equivalent to نِ (n-i, “without, -less”) + ڈَر (ḍar, “fear”). Cognates Cognate with Punjabi نِڈَر (niḍar) / ਨਿਡਰ (niḍar), Sindhi نِڋَرُ (niḍ̠aru), Nepali निडर (niḍar) and Gujarati નીડર (nīḍar).

نرخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نرخ (nirx). Compare Bengali নিরিখ (nirikh), Malayalam നിരക്ക് (nirakkŭ).

senses.g: a female given name, Nargis, from Persian
a kind of narcissus of which the flowers have black centres instead of yellow ones, resembling eye pupils

نرگس شہلا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَرْگِسِ شَهْلَا (nargis-i šahlā).

نرمی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَرْمِی (narmī). By surface analysis, نَرْم (narm) + ـی (-ī).

نروان.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit निर्वाण (nirvāṇa, “blown or put out, extinguished”).
Borrowed from Sanskrit निर्वाण (nirvāṇa, “blown or put out, extinguished”).

نروگ.e: Derived from Prakrit 𑀡𑀻𑀭𑁄𑀕 (ṇīroga) from Sanskrit नीरोग (nīroga).

نزہت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نُزْهَت (nuzhat), from Arabic نُزْهَة (nuzha).

نس.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀡𑀲𑀸 (ṇasā), from Sanskrit स्नसा (snasā).
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀦𑀺𑀲𑀺 (nisi), from Sanskrit निशा (niśā).

نسا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نسا (nisā), from Arabic نِسَاء (nisāʔ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian نسا (nisā), from Arabic نِسَاء (nisāʔ).
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀦𑀺𑀲𑀸 (nisā), from Sanskrit निशा (niśā). Doublet of نِس (nis) and نِشا (niśā).

نسبتا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نِسْبَتاً (nisbatan), from Arabic نِسْبَةً (nisbatan).

نستعلیق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نستعلیق (nasta'līq), of Arabic origin.

نسخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَسْخ (nasx), borrowed from Arabic نَسْخ (nasḵ).

senses.g: prescription (for medicine or of medical notes)

نسخہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نُسْخَه (nusxa), borrowed from Arabic نُسْخَة (nusḵa).

senses.g: naswar, a kind of moist, powdered tobacco snuff
a gentle breeze; morning breeze; zephyr

ex.u: نفرت اور محبت کی اس کشاکش کو دیکھنا ہے تو ایک گُل کو دیکھو جس کے ابھرنے اور کھلنے میں نسیم کی عطر بیزی کے ساتھ سورج کی جاں فزائی بھی شامل ہے۔
ex.e: If you wish to see the perplexity of love and hate, then observe a flower, for in its rising and blooming involves the fragrant play of the breeze and the life-giving warmth of the sun.

نسیم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَسِیم (nasīm), borrowed from Arabic نَسِيم (nasīm). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi نسیم (nsym /⁠nasīm⁠/).

senses.g: a unisex given name, Naseem, from Arabic

نسیم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَسِیم (nasīm), borrowed from Arabic نَسِيم (nasīm). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi نسیم (nsym /⁠nasīm⁠/).

senses.g: memorial, mark of remembrance, keepsake, bookmark
scattering, spreading, expanding, extending, diffusing, dispersing
broadcast station (particularly a radio station or television station)
retransmission, rebroadcast, a repeated or relayed broadcast

نشست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَشِسْت (nišast), from نشستن (nišastan, “to sit down”).

نشور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نُشُور (nušūr), from Arabic نُشُور (nušūr).

senses.g: a male given name, Nushur or Nushoor, from Arabic

نشور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نُشُور (nušūr), from Arabic نُشُور (nušūr).

senses.g: intoxicants, psychoactive substances; alcohol, alcoholic beverage, drug, narcotic

نشہ.e: Derived from Classical Persian نشئه (naš'a)/نشا (našâ), from Arabic نَشْأَة (našʔa). Compare Assamese নিচা (nisa), Bengali নেশা (neśa), Gujarati નશો (naśo).

ex.u: گندے لوگوں کو خدا کی نصرت کبھی نہیں ملتی۔
ex.r: gande logõ ko xudā kī nusarat kabhi nahĩ milti.
ex.e: The evil ones never obtain help from God.

نصرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نصرت (nusrat), from Arabic نُصْرَة (nuṣra).

نصف النہار.e: Borrowed from Arabic نِصْف النَّهَار (niṣf an-nahār).

نصیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نصیب (“portion, share, lot”), from Arabic نَصِيب (naṣīb).

نصیحت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَصیحَت (nasēhat), from Arabic نَصِيحَة (naṣīḥa).

نظام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نظام (nizām), from Arabic نِظَام (niẓām).

نظام شمسی.e: Borrowed from Arabic نِظَام شَمْسِيّ (niẓām šamsiyy).

نظامت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نظامت (nizāmat), from Arabic نِظامَة (niẓāma).

ex.u: آپ کی نَظَر خَراب ہوتی جا رَہی ہَیں۔
ex.r: āp kī nazar xarāb hotī jā rahī ha͠i.

ex.u: اُن کی نَظَر مَیں یہ غَلَط ہَے
ex.r: un kī nazar ma͠i ye ġalat hai
ex.e: In their view, this is wrong

نظر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نظر (nazar), from Arabic نَظَر (naẓar).

senses.g: reconsideration, review (such as of a decision), second opinion

نظر ثانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نظر ثانی (nazar-i sānī). Compare Punjabi ਨਜ਼ਰਸਾਨੀ (nazarsānī) / نَظَرثانی (naz̤ars̱ānī), Sindhi نظر ثاني (̃z̤r s̱āne), Gujarati નજરસાની (najarsānī).

senses.g: out of sight, out of mind (gone and forgotten)

نظر سے دور، دل کے قریب.h: नज़र से दूर, दिल के क़रीब

نظر نواز.e: From نظر (nazar, “gaze”) + ـنواز (navāz, “(forms adjectives and nouns)”).

نظریہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَظَرِیَّه (nazariyya), from Arabic نَظَرِيَّة (naẓariyya).

ex.u: قرآن مجید لفظ بلفظ آنحضرت صلی اللہ علیہ وآلہٰ وسلم کے قلب پر القا ہوا تھا اور وہی الفاظ اور اسی نظم سے جس طرح القا ہوئے تھے آنحضرت صلی اللہ علیہ وآلہٰ وسلم نے لوگوں کو پڑھ سنایا۔
ex.e: The Holy Qur'an was inculcated in the heart of the Holy Prophet, peace and blessings of Allah be upon him, word for word, and in the same words and in the same order as they were inculcated, were recited by the Holy Prophet, peace and blessings of Allah be upon him, to the people.

ex.u: میں لکچر سے پہلے تبرکاً اپنی نظم پڑھ لیا کرتا ہوں اگرچہ وہ نظم بودی پُھسپُھسی اور نامربوط سی ہوتی ہؠ۔
ex.e: I [tend] to read my poetry before my lecture, although that [piece of] poetry usually [tends] to be weak, mild and inconsistent.

ex.u: یہ سن کر انہوں نے کہا "ٹھیک ہے مگر جماعت کا نظم بھی کوئی چیز ہے!"
ex.e: Upon hearing this he said, "All right, but there is such thing as the community's discipline!"

senses.g: threading, stringing (of a needle, pearl etc.)

ex.u: اس نے فکر اور عقل کے دریا سے نہایت نادر و نایاب موتی ڈھونڈ نکالے اور انھیں نظم کے ہار میں چابک دستی سے پرویا۔
ex.e: With [great] care and wisdom, he discovered extremely rare and precious pearls from the river which he filled the necklace with skill, in the same threading.

نظم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَظْم (nazm), from Arabic نَظْم (naẓm). First attested as Middle Hindi نظم (nz̤m), from the Arabic root ن ظ م (n ẓ m). See other Urdu terms derived from the Arabic root ن ظ م.

نظیف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نظیف (nazīf), from Arabic نَظِيف (naẓīf).

senses.g: naat, poem in praise of Holy Prophet Muhammad.

نعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَعْت (na't), from Arabic نَعْت (naʕt).

نعرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نعره (na're, “lion's roar”), from Arabic نَعْرَة (naʕra, “grunt; roar”).

senses.g: better alternative, change for the better (especially, but not limited to, after incurring a loss)

ex.u: نوجوان عورت کا بچّہ مرجائے تو اُس کو خدا سے نعم البدل ملنے کی امید رکھنی چاہیے۔
ex.e: If a young woman's child dies, she should hope to receive a better return from God.

ex.u: ایک شعبہ ایسا ہے جس میں معلوم ہوتا ہے کہ مہارت ختم ہو گئی ہے اور اس کا ہمیں کوئی نعم البدل نہیں ملا ہے۔
ex.e: There is a department in which expertise seems to have diminished and we have had no such replacement.

نعمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نِعْمَت (ni'mat), taken from Arabic نِعْمَة (niʕma).

ex.u: محبت کے نغمات گائیں گے ہم۔
ex.r: muhabbat ke naġmāt gāēn ge ham.
ex.e: We will recite the melodies of love.

نغمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نغمه (naġma), itself from Arabic نَغْمة (naḡma).

senses.g: a female given name, Naghma, from Arabic

نغمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نغمه (naġma), itself from Arabic نَغْمة (naḡma).

senses.g: inflection of نَغْمَہ (naġma, “melody; tune”): — direct plural
inflection of نَغْمَہ (naġma, “melody; tune”): — oblique/vocative singular

نفت.e: From Classical Persian نَفْت (naft), from Middle Persian [script needed] (npt' /⁠naft⁠/, “moist, damp; naphtha”), from Old Persian *naftah (“naphtha”).

نفرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَفْرَت (nafrat), from Arabic نَفْرَة (nafra).

senses.g: to hate, to abhor, to detest

ex.u: مَیں تُم سے نَفْرَت کَرْتا ہُوں۔
ex.r: ma͠i tum se nafrat kartā hū̃.

نقارہ.e: Learned borrowing from Arabic نَقَّارَة (naqqāra). Doublet of نَگَاڑَا (nagāṛā).

نقاشی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَقَّاشِی (naqqāšī). By surface analysis, نَقَّاش (naqqāś, “painter”) + ـی (-ī).

نقشہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نقشه (naqša), taken from Arabic نَقْش (naqš, “painting, figure”).

نقطہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نقطه (nuqta), from Arabic نُقْطَة (nuqṭa).

نقل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَقْل (naql), borrowed from Arabic نَقْل (naql).

نقل حرفی.e: From نقل (naqal, “copy”) + حرف (ḥarf, “letter”) + ی (ī), literally “copying of lettering”. Compare Arabic نَقْحَر (naqḥar).

نقل نگاری.e: From نقل (naqal, “copy”) + نگار (nigār, “work”) + ی (ī), literally “copying of work”.

نقلی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نقلی (naqlī). Equivalent to نقل (naql, “imitation”) + ی (ī).

نکاح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نِکَاح (nikāh), from Arabic نِكَاح (nikāḥ).

نکالنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀺𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀸𑀮𑁂𑀤𑀺 (ṇikkāledi), from Sanskrit निष्कालयति (niṣkālayati). Compare Sindhi نِڪارَڻُ (nikāraṇu) / निकारणु, Nepali निकाल्नु (nikālnu), Marathi निखळणे (nikhaḷṇe).

senses.g: useless, worthless; unemployed, good-for-nothing; idle; unserviceable; without work

نکما.e: Borrowed from Punjabi نِکَمّا (nikammā). Doublet of نِکام (nikām).

نگاڑا.e: Ultimately derived from Arabic نَقَّارَة (naqqāra), with the widespread change in Indo-Aryan descendants qq > g suggesting an intermediary. Cognate with Punjabi ਨਗਾਰਾ (nagārā), ਨਗਾੜਾ (nagāṛā), Nepali नगरा (nagarā), Odia ନାଗରା (nāgarā). Doublet of نَقَّارَہ (naqqāra).

نگاہ.e: First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi نگاہ (nigāh), borrowed from Classical Persian نِگَاه (nigāh).

نگرانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نگرانی (nigrânî). Equivalent of نگران (nigrān) + ـی (-ī).

نماز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَمَاز (namāz). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi نماز (namāz). Doublet of نَمَسْتے (namaste).

نمائندگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نُمَایَنْدَگِی (numāyandagī). By surface analysis, نُمائِنْدَہ (numāinda, “representative”) + گی (gī).

senses.g: A salutation; a greeting used for most purposes including hello, good morning/afternoon, and goodbye
greeting, salutation, an instance of the interjection namaste

نمک حلال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَمَک حَلَال (namak halāl).

نند.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi نند (nand /⁠nanad⁠/) / ننند (nanad), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀡𑀁𑀤𑀸 (ṇaṇaṃdā), from Sanskrit ननान्दृ (nánāndṛ), ननन्दृ (nanandṛ).

senses.g: husband of husband's sister – masculine of نَنْد (nand, “husband's sister”)

نندوئی.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀡𑀡𑀁𑀤𑀯𑀇 (*ṇaṇaṃdavaï) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit ननान्दृपति (nanāndṛpati).

senses.g: vocative plural of نَنْدوئی (nandoī, “husband of husband's sister”)
oblique plural of نَنْدوئی (nandoī, “husband of husband's sister”)

ننگا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀡𑀕𑁆𑀕 (ṇagga), from Sanskrit नग्न (nagná), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *nagnás.

ننھا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi नन्हा (nanhā), नान्हा (nānhā), from Prakrit 𑀮𑀡𑁆𑀳 (laṇha) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit श्लक्ष्ण (ślakṣṇá, “soft, smooth”).

نو.e: Inherited from Old Hindi नौ (nau), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀯 (ṇava), from Sanskrit नव (náva).
Borrowed from Classical Persian نو (naw). Doublet of نیا (nayā).

نواب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَوَّاب (nawwāb), from Arabic نُوَّاب (nuwwāb), plural of نَائِب (nāʔib).

senses.g: Nawabshah (a city in Shaheed Benazirabad district, Sindh, Pakistan)
colonial, relating to colonialism or colonies
condition, circumstances, period (favourable for something to happen); a chance

ex.u: ہماری جیت کی نوبت اب آ گئی ہے۔
ex.r: hamārī jīt kī naubat ab ā gaī hai.
ex.e: The conditions favourable for our victory have arrived.

نوبت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نوبت (nawbat), from Arabic نَوْبَة (nawba).

نوجوان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَوجَوَان (nawjawān). By surface analysis, نَو (nau, “new”) + جَوَان (javān, “young person”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian نَوجَوَان (nawjawān). By surface analysis, نَو (nau, “new”) + جَوَان (javān, “young person”).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

نوح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نوح (nūḥ), from Arabic نُوح (nūḥ).

نور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نُور (nūr), from Arabic نُور (nūr). Compare Bengali নূর (nur).

senses.g: a unisex given name, Noor, from Arabic

نور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نُور (nūr), from Arabic نُور (nūr). Compare Bengali নূর (nur).

نوش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نوش (nôš), derived from Middle Persian 𐭠𐭭𐭥𐭱 (anōš, “elixir, antidote”), from Old Persian *anauša- (“immortal”), ultimately from Proto-Iranian *an-auša- (“immortal”).

senses.g: Nushki (a city in Nushki district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
Nowshera (a city in Nowshera district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)

نوشہرہ.e: From نَو (nau, “new”) + شہر (śahar, “city”), possibly a calque of Pashto نوښار (nowx̌ār) or vice versa.

نوشی.e: Derived from Classical Persian نوش (nōš, “a drink”).

نوکرانی.e: From Classical Persian نوکر (nawkar) + Sanskrit feminine suffix -आनी (-ānī).

نوکری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نوکری (nawkarī). Equivalent to نوکر (nawkar, “servant, employee”) + ـی (-ī).

نومبر.e: Borrowed from English November, ultimately from Latin November.

نونہال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَونِهَال (nawnihāl). By surface analysis, نَو (nau, “new”) + نِہَال (nihāl, “sapling”). First attested in c. 1685 as Middle Hindi نونہال (nvnhal /⁠naunihāl⁠/).

نوید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نوید (nawēd, nuwēd), from Middle Persian nwyk' (*niwē), from Proto-Iranian *wayd-. Compare Manichaean Parthian nwydg (niwēdag, “good news”). Related to Parthian nwyst (niwist, “invited”).

senses.g: a male given name, Navid, Naveed, or Naved, from Persian

نوید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نوید (nawēd, nuwēd), from Middle Persian nwyk' (*niwē), from Proto-Iranian *wayd-. Compare Manichaean Parthian nwydg (niwēdag, “good news”). Related to Parthian nwyst (niwist, “invited”).

ex.u: وزیرِ اعظم سے ہمارے ایک نِویدَن ہے.
ex.e: We have a request for Prime minister

نویسندہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَوِیسَنْدَه (navīsanda, “writer; author”).

senses.g: used in rhetorical phrases: isn't it, -n't, is it not (he/she/it/they)

ex.u: میرا نام بہت لمبا ہے، نہ؟
ex.r: merā nām bahut lambā hai, na?
ex.e: My name is very long, isn't it?

نہ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡 (ṇa), from Sanskrit न (na), likely influenced by Classical Persian نه (na). Doublet of نا (nā).

ex.u: نہ وہ کھیل دیکھنے گئے نہ ہم۔
ex.r: nā vo khel dekhne ga'e nā ham.
ex.e: Neither they nor we went to see the game.

نہ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡 (ṇa), from Sanskrit न (na), likely influenced by Classical Persian نه (na). Doublet of نا (nā).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡 (ṇa), from Sanskrit न (na), likely influenced by Classical Persian نه (na). Doublet of نا (nā).

نہاد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نِهَاد (nihād, “disposition, nature”).

نہایت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نهایت (nihāyat), from Arabic نِهَايَة (nihāya).
Borrowed from Classical Persian نهایت (nihāyat), from Arabic نِهَايَة (nihāya).
Borrowed from Classical Persian نهایت (nihāyat), from Arabic نِهَايَة (nihāya).

senses.g: inflection of نِہَتّا (nihattā, “unarmed, weaponless”): — direct masculine plural
inflection of نِہَتّا (nihattā, “unarmed, weaponless”): — oblique masculine singular/plural
a rising up; marching (against); departure, march

نہضت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نهضت (nahzat), from Arabic نَهْضَة (nahḍa).

نہیں.e: Inherited from Sanskrit नहि (nahí, “surely not”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *naǰʰí, from earlier *ná gʲʰí, from Proto-Indo-European *né + *gʰí. Cognate with Bengali নাই (nai)/নেই (nei), Early Assamese নাহি (nahi), Assamese নাই (nai), Braj नहीं (nahī̃).

Inherited from Sanskrit नहि (nahí, “surely not”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *naǰʰí, from earlier *ná gʲʰí, from Proto-Indo-European *né + *gʰí. Cognate with Bengali নাই (nai)/নেই (nei), Early Assamese নাহি (nahi), Assamese নাই (nai), Braj नहीं (nahī̃).

نیا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀯𑀺𑀅 (ṇavia), from Sanskrit नविय (náviya), नव्य (návya) (whence Bhojpuri नया (nayā)), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *náwyas, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *náwyas (whence Kamkata-viri nuĩ), from Proto-Indo-European *néwyos (whence English new). Doublet of نو (no). Cognates include Punjabi نَوَاں / ਨਵਾਂ (navā̃) and Nepali नयाँ (nayā̃).

senses.g: Nepal (a country in South Asia, located between China and India)
a transliteration of the Hebrew surname נְתַנְיָהוּ (Netanyahu)

نیٹرون.e: Borrowed from English natron, from French natron, from Arabic نطرون

نیچا.e: Derived from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀺𑀘𑁆𑀘 (ṇicca) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit नीच (nīcá), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *niHćás (“facing down”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *niHčás, from Proto-Indo-European *ni-h₃kʷ-ós, from *ní (“down”) + *-h₃kʷ- (“facing, looking”).

senses.g: Netherlands (a country in Western Europe, consisting of four constituent countries: the Netherlands per se, Aruba, Curaçao and Sint Maarten the Kingdom of the Netherlands)
Netherlands (the main constituent country of the Kingdom of the Netherlands, located primarily in Western Europe bordering Germany and Belgium)

نیزہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نیزَه (nēza). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi نیزہ (neza).

senses.g: good, virtuous, righteous, upright, pious, kind-hearted, mannerly
